content.decision_question: Should quality investment in comprehensive schools be pursued as a precondition for structural reform, as a standalone strategy, or as an insufficient substitute that risks leaving segregation architecture untouched indefinitely?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can measure whether quality investment changes enrollment patterns and reduces demand for selective tracks over time, and can quantify teacher-shortage gaps and school-closure risk. It cannot settle whether status-signaling motives for track-seeking are more fundamental than quality concerns, nor can it resolve the values question of whether alleviating current harm takes priority over dismantling the sorting structure that causes it.
content.tension: Investing in comprehensive-school quality is the least politically contentious and legally simplest option, and addresses documented resource disparities, but it does not touch the selection mechanism itself, and demand for selective tracks may be driven by status-signaling rather than quality perception, raising doubt about whether it can substitute for structural reform rather than merely complement it.
content.title: Compensatory investment versus structural detracking as the primary equity lever
value_poles: reducing harm to students in the existing system now
transforming the sorting mechanism that produces segregation
